1: Va-yomer YHWH elai -- 'Then the LORD said to me.' Ch 3 is the prose companion to Hosea 1-2's prophetic poetry: Hosea's renewed experience of redemption mirrors YHWH's covenant renewal. Lekh ahav ishah ahuvat rea ve-naafet -- 'Go again, love a woman who is loved by another man and is an adulteress.' Ahav (H157, to love) is the command -- love is not merely feeling but a covenantal act of will. The woman is simultaneously loved by another (rea, a companion/friend) and an adulteress (naafet). Ka-ahavat YHWH et-benei Yisrael ve-hem penim el-elohim acherim -- 'just as YHWH loves the children of Israel, though they turn to other gods.' The analogy is explicit: Hosea's continuing love for the unfaithful Gomer enacts YHWH's continuing love for unfaithful Israel. Love is not conditional on faithfulness; it persists through the faithlessness.
2: Va-ekrehah li bi-asah asar keseph -- 'So I bought her back for fifteen pieces of silver.' Karah (H3739, to buy/acquire) -- a commercial term for purchasing or hire. The price of fifteen pieces of silver plus agricultural goods (a homer and a half of barley) is approximately half the slave price (thirty pieces of silver, Exodus 21:32). The exact amount has been interpreted as indicating that Gomer had fallen into debt-slavery or cult prostitution and Hosea redeemed her at the going market rate. The redemption price -- less than a slave's value -- anticipates the humiliated state of Israel in exile and YHWH's redemption of a people who have become worthless by covenant violation.
3: Va-omar eleha yamim rabbim teshevi li -- 'And I said to her, You must stay with me for many days.' The terms of the restored relationship are strict: no prostitution (lo tiznei), no intercourse with another (ve-lo tihyi le-ish), and -- remarkably -- abstinence from Hosea himself (ve-gam anokhi elayikh). The restoration requires a period of waiting and purification before full marital relations are restored. This mirrors the prophetic word about Israel's period of exile: many days without king, prince, sacrifice, pillar -- the institutions of national life suspended during the purification. The waiting is not abandonment but the preparation for full restoration.
4: Ki yamim rabbim yeshvu benei-Yisrael -- 'For the children of Israel will live for many days without a king, without a ruler, without a sacrifice, without a pillar, without an ephod or teraphim.' The Hosea-Gomer waiting period is the key to the verse: the suspension of all Israel's political and cultic institutions parallels the suspension of the marital relationship. Melekh ve-sar (king and prince) -- the political institutions. Zevach ve-matzevah (sacrifice and pillar) -- the cultic institutions, including both legitimate (zevach) and syncretistic (matzevah, sacred pillar). Efod ve-terafim (ephod and teraphim) -- oracular objects, both legitimate and illegitimate. The exile strips Israel of both covenant-proper and syncretistic religion -- a total suspension that prepares for the purified restoration.
5: Acharei yashuvu benei-Yisrael -- 'Afterward the children of Israel will return.' Shub (H7725, to return/repent/turn back) -- the key word for both physical return from exile and spiritual repentance. U-viqshu et-YHWH Eloheihem ve-et David malkam -- 'and seek the LORD their God and David their king.' The messianic expectation: a future Davidic king who unites the people under YHWH (cf. Jeremiah 30:9; Ezekiel 34:23-24; Hosea 37:24-25). 'David their king' cannot refer to the historical David (long dead); it points to the expected Davidic heir who will restore the united kingdom. The NT identification: Jesus of the house of David, born in Bethlehem. Ve-pachadu el-YHWH ve-el-tuvo be-acharit ha-yamim -- 'they will come trembling to the LORD and to his goodness in the latter days.' Acharit ha-yamim (the latter days/the end of days) -- the eschatological frame: this is not merely a historical restoration but the final-age return.
